 ONE SENTEN CE SUMMARY 30 
Patient -derived immunocompetent cultures uncover responses to immunotherapy combinations to guide 31 
precision oncology in ovarian cancer. 32 
 33 
SHORT TITLE 34 
iPDCs for precision immuno -oncology in ovarian cancer . 35

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
3 
 ABSTRACT 36 
Identifying effective immunotherapies requires testing in pre -clinical models that represent the patient -specific 37 
immune microenvironment. Here, we established a unique patient -derived immunocompetent culture (iPDC) 38 
model for high -grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) cultured on a novel physiologically relevant patient - 39 
derived matrix. We validate our fu nctional immuno -oncology platform using histological, genomic and single - 40 
cell analysis, and show that the iPDC responses significantly correlate with the patient’s clinical responses and 41 
progression free survival. We identify new therapeutic options associ ated with distinct tumor cells states in 42 
relapsed, therapy resistant HGSC patients. Using h igh-throughput drug testing followed by single cell -imaging, 43 
we discover ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3 -related inhibitor (ATRi) combined with a novel immunotherapy 44 
targeting Autotaxin (ATX) as a promising new combination treatment for HGSC. The iPDC responses to ATRi 45 
plus ATX inhibitor associate with immune cell activation and a distinct source tumor biomarker profile. In 46 
conclusion, iPDCs serve as a unique, high-throughput and functional platform for testing immunotherapy 47 
combinations and predictive biomarker discovery for precision oncology in HGSC 48

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
4 
 INTRODUCTION 49 
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is the most common subtype of ovarian cancer, often diagnosed at an 50 
advanced stage with 10 -year survival of 15% (1). Nearly 80% of the HGSC metastasize to omentum, a large 51 
fat tissue covering stomach and intestine, which provides metabolic and inflammatory microenvironment for 52 
cancer and immune cells (2). The s tandard of care for HGSC includes primary debulking surgery followed by 53 
platinum -based chemotherapy . Despite the successful response to initial treatment, recurrent disease often 54 
develops within the first three years . Poly (ADP -ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have improved patient’s 55 
outcome when used as a maintenance treatment after first -line chemotherapy, particularly in patients with 56 
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), and or sensitivity to platinum -based chemotherapy (3). 57 
However , 35-45% of the patients discontinue the treatment due to innate or acquired resistance to PARPi (4, 58 
5), suggesting that novel combination strategies are required to overcome resistance to PARPi . 59 
Single agent immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have shown limited efficacy in HGSC with overall response 60 
rate of 10% (6). The l ack of success with ICIs in HGSC is likely due to a variety of immunosuppressive cells 61 
and mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME) (7).

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
sponse 60 
rate of 10% (6). The l ack of success with ICIs in HGSC is likely due to a variety of immunosuppressive cells 61 
and mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME) (7). Targeting immunosuppressive mechanisms 62 
operated by myeloid cells is one of the strategies to improve the efficacy of ICIs. Towards this, studies from 63 
mouse models show the importance of targeting Autot axin-Lysophosphatidic acid (LPA ) pathway or PKR -like 64 
endoplasmic -reticulum (ER) kinase (PERK) to enhance CD8+ T cell response via activating interferon 65 
signaling in dendritic cells (DCs) or by depleting myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSC), respectively (8, 9). 66 
To improve the therapeutic benefit of ICI s, their combination with chemotherapy, PARPi, or anti -angiogenic 67 
agents are under clinical investigation (10, 11 ). Interestingly, results from the TOPACIO Phase I/II clinical trial 68 
showed that mutational signature 3 as a surrogate of HRD status predicts sensitivity to combination of PAR Pi, 69 
niraparib and anti -PD1 antibody, pembrolizumab (12). In addition, BRCA 1/2 defective HGSC has been shown 70 
to exhibit increased immunosurveillance with more neoantigens, tumor infiltrating lymphocytes, programmed 71 
cell death (PD -1) and its ligand (PD -L1) compared to HRP tumors (13-15). These findings highlight the 72

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
e with more neoantigens, tumor infiltrating lymphocytes, programmed 71 
cell death (PD -1) and its ligand (PD -L1) compared to HRP tumors (13-15). These findings highlight the 72 
investigation of novel immunotherapy combinations for patients with HGSC . Various agents inhibiting DNA 73 
damag e response (DDR i) including cell cycle checkpoint inhibitors targeting ATR or WEE1 kinases are being 74 
clinically explored (16). While preclinical studies implicate benefit of combin ing of DDR i and IC Is (17), 75 
investigation of novel combinations of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents or immunotherapies is required 76 
to guide personalized immuno -oncology in ovarian cancer. However, prior to clinical investigation of novel 77

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
5 
 immunotherapies, the efficacy of these agents needs to be validated in immunocompetent models representing 78 
patient tumor -specific immune microenvironment . 79 
A variety of patient -derived ex vivo models have been developed to model tumor immune microenvironment, 80 
and to evaluate response to immunotherapies (18-20). All these studies have utilized matrigel or collagen - 81 
based matrices to culture the cells or tissue fragments. However, the source of these matrices being commonly 82 
of mouse or rat origin, do not represent HGSC patient TME. In addition, previous studies using patient -derived 83 
models have primarily focused on the e ffect of ICIs on functional responses from immune cells but not the 84 
cytotoxic response from tumor cells. Furthermore, these models are not suitable for high -throughput 85 
combinatorial drug testing. Human tissue -specific extracellular matri ces (ECM) have been produced to model 86 
the primary and metastatic TME (21, 22). Towards this, Neilson et al., have generated and characterized a 87 
human omentum gel matrix (OmGel) from tumor -free omentum , which has the potential utility for studying 88 
omental metastatic microenvironment across a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to oment um 89 
(23). Here we established a functional precision immuno -oncology platform using novel HGSC patient -derived 90

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to oment um 89 
(23). Here we established a functional precision immuno -oncology platform using novel HGSC patient -derived 90 
immunocompetent cultures (iPDCs) using patient -derived OmGel matrix . We validated that the iPDCs 91 
preserve tumor and immune cell features of the native tumor and recapitulate the clinical responses to 92 
chemotherapy and PARPis. Importantly, by coupling single cell image -based drug responses with immune cell 93 
functional states we show that iPDCs reveal patient -specific vulnerabilities to novel immuno -oncology and 94 
combination therap ies, which associate with distinct tissue biomarkers in HGSC. 95

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
6 
 RESULTS 96 
Patient -derived novel OmGel matrix significantly improves the growth of HGSC patient -derived 97 
cultures that are applicable for drug testing. 98 
In this study, we have processed 4 7 HGSC tumors from 44 patients and established cultures from 3 7 tumors 99 
(Table. S, 1 ). We first started with optimizing tumor tissue dissociation , as the e nzymatic dissociation is a 100 
necessary step in setting up ex vivo cultures and can influence the successful establishment of patient tumor - 101 
derived cultures. We compared two commonly used tissue dissociating enzymes: Collagenase/Hyaluronidase 102 
(C&H), and dispase for the final yield, viability, abundance of tumor and immune cells, and e xpression of stress 103 
response genes ( Fig. S1, A). Adjacent tissue regions from each tumor were dissociated with C&H or dispase. 104 
Our results showed no significant difference in the final yield or calcein positive live cells between C&H and 105 
dispase -dissociation ( Fig. S1, B-D). Similarly, the abundance of CK7+ tumor cells or CD45+ immune cells 106 
were not affected by the nature of enzymes ( Fig. S1, E and F). However, the expressions of stress response 107 
genes ATF3 , FOS, and HSPA1 were increased in C&H dissociated cells compared to dispase ( Fig. S1, G). 108

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
s ( Fig. S1, E and F). However, the expressions of stress response 107 
genes ATF3 , FOS, and HSPA1 were increased in C&H dissociated cells compared to dispase ( Fig. S1, G). 108 
The upregulation of stress response genes has been linked to chemoresistance (24, 25 ) and therefore can 109 
distort drug response analys is from cancer cells. Hence, we continued with dispase -dissociation for 110 
establishing iPDCs ( Fig. 1, A). HGSC primary or omental metastatic tumor -dissociated cells were cultured in 111 
basement membrane extract (BME) or OmGel for up to one week. Compared to cells cultured in BME, OmGel 112 
cultured cells showed significantly improved growth as measured by the number of PDCs with size > 3000 µm2 113 
(Fig. 1, B, C, and S1. H ). Importantly, OmGel cultured PDCs maintained source tumor morp hology and 114 
expression of tumor -specific marker PAX8 (Fig. 1, D and E). In addition, whole exome sequencing (WES) 115 
analysis of tumor and matched PDCs (n=10) showed strong correlation (r=0.86) for the variant allelic 116 
frequencies (VAF), and a high median concordance (95.8%) for tumor -specific mutations between tumor and 117 
PDC pairs ( Fig. 1, F- G, Table . S, 2). 118 
Next, we examined whether OmGel cultured PDCs c ould be utilized for drug response analysis. Clinically 119

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
s between tumor and 117 
PDC pairs ( Fig. 1, F- G, Table . S, 2). 118 
Next, we examined whether OmGel cultured PDCs c ould be utilized for drug response analysis. Clinically 119 
PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy is effective particularly in HGSC patients with HRD and or BRCA mutation 120 
(26). Howe ver, nearly 30% of the patients with a germline BRCA mutation relapse within the first 2 years on a 121 
PARPi maintenance therapy (27). We tested if the PDCs established from treatment -naive patient -derived 122 
tumors exhibit HRD genotype -specific response s to PARPi. All the tumor samples used in this study were 123 
subjected to the genomic ovaHRD scar test to evaluate homologs recombination ( HR) status (Table . S, 3) (28). 124

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
7 
 We treated the treatment naive HRD (n=11) or HRP (n=8) patient tumor derived PDCs with olaparib for up to 125 
one week and evaluated the response as a decrease in the proportion of live CK7+ tumor cells normalized to 126 
the control . The result showed that the tumor cells from HRD -PDCs responded significantly better compared 127 
to HRP -PDCs (Fig. 1 , H and I ). Importantly, PDC tumor cell response to olaparib significantly correlated with 128 
the progression -free survival (PFS) of the corresponding patients as well as the duration of PARPi treatment 129 
in these patients (Fig. 1, J and K) . In summary, these results show that novel OmGel matrix enables the 130 
successful generation of HGSC PDCs that are amenable for dr ug response analysis, which significantly 131 
correlate with the patient’s PFS and clinical responses to PARPi treatment . 132 
Patient -derived immunocompetent cultures maintain native immune cells and enable the investigation 133 
of immune cell -specific functional resp onses to immuno -oncology agents . 134 
To evaluate if the nature of the enzyme or culturing matrix influence on the recovery of immune cell type s, we 135 
performed multi -parameter flow cytometry on C&H or dispase -dissociated cells or cells cultured in BME or 136 
OmGel. The populations negative for dead cell marker and epithelial cells (DCM- EpCAM-), were gated in 137

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
y on C&H or dispase -dissociated cells or cells cultured in BME or 136 
OmGel. The populations negative for dead cell marker and epithelial cells (DCM- EpCAM-), were gated in 137 
FlowJO and further analyzed using CYTO (29) (Fig. 2, A and Fig. S2, A). The DCM- EpCAM- data from all the 138 
samples comparing enzymes or matrix conditions were clustered into 25 d ifferent clusters using FlowSOM, 139 
and annotated as T cell, myeloid, or other clusters. The data from these clusters were further analyzed using 140 
CYTO and annotated as different immune cell types ( Fig. S2, A). Comparison of the immune cell proportions 141 
between C&H -vs dispase -dissociation or between BME vs OmGel cultures did not show statistically significant 142 
difference for any of the cell types tested using paired Wilcoxon signed rank test ( Fig. S2, B and C ). 143 
Next, we assessed the presence of source tumor -specific immune cells in OmGel cultures. We performed 144 
multi -parameter flow cytometry on freshly -dissociate d tumor cells (tumor) and matched PDCs cultured for 4 – 145 
7-days and analyzed the data similarly as before (Fig. 2, A and S2, A ). The results showed that the proportions 146 
of the cell types varied between tumor and corresponding PDCs ; but this difference was not statistically 147 
significant, as tested using paired Wilcoxon signed rank test.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
s 146 
of the cell types varied between tumor and corresponding PDCs ; but this difference was not statistically 147 
significant, as tested using paired Wilcoxon signed rank test. Importantly, T cells, NK cells and dendritic cells 148 
required for eliciting response to immunotherapies were maintained in the cultures ( Fig. 2, B). Moreover, 149 
immunofluorescence staining of selected immune cell markers showed not only the presence of CD45+ 150 
immune cells, CD8+ T cells, CD68+ and CD163+ macrophages in the cultures but also spatial interaction 151 
between immune cells and CK7+ tumor cells ( Fig. 2, C). Together, these results validate that HGSC iPDCs 152 
preserve source tumor -specific immune cells. 153

 To assess the immune -cell functional states after treatments, we performed single -cell multi -parameter flow 154 
cytometry analysis for dendritic cells, T cells and NK cell s functional markers such as HLA-Dr, interferon 155 
gamma ( IFNγ ), granzymenB ( GrzB ) and Ki67 in 11 iPDCs treated with olaparib or pembrolizumab. The DCM- 156 
EpCAM- cells were gated in FlowJO and further analyzed using CYTO. (Fig. S2 E ). We observed patient - 157 
specific immune cell activation evidenced by increase in HLA -Dr, IFNγ , or Ki67 in dendritic cells or GrzB, IFNγ, 158 
or Ki67 in T cells and NK cells. Notably, pembrolizumab treatment increased antigen presentation of dendritic 159 
cells predominantly in HRD iPDCs (4/6 HRD iPDCs vs 1/5 HRP iPDCs), while CD8+ T cell activation was 160 
independent of the tumor HRD status ( Fig. 2, D ). In addition, following pembrolizumab treatment, CD4+ T cells 161 
and CD56+ NK cells showed an increase in IFNγ particularly in HRD -iPDCs. Next, we investigated tumor cell 162 
responses from iPDCs upon pembrolizumab tr eatment via single cell image -analysis measuring the 163 
abundance of CK7+ tumor cells. We observed that 3/4 HRD iPDCs, but none of the HRP -iPDCs responded to 164 
pembrolizumab treatment ( Fig. S2 F and G) . In summary, these results show that HRD tumors exhibit 165

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
observed that 3/4 HRD iPDCs, but none of the HRP -iPDCs responded to 164 
pembrolizumab treatment ( Fig. S2 F and G) . In summary, these results show that HRD tumors exhibit 165 
increased antigen presentation and CD 4+T and NK cell activation associated with tumor cell responses to 166 
pembrolizumab treatment, highlighting the potential of the iPDC platform to reveal both functional cell state 167 
dynamics and cytotoxic responses to immunothe rapies. 168 
iPDCs recapitulate clinical response to olaparib or chemotherapy and reveal drug sensitivities 169 
correlating with distinct epithelial cell pathway activities in the resistant tumors. 170 
To further establish how iPDC -based drug responses could reveal new therapeutic options , we processed 171 
tumors from three patients whose disease had progressed during olaparib or chemotherapy treatment , and 172 
subjected the tumors for genomic and transcriptomic analyses , and drug testing on iPDCs ( Fig. 3, A and 173 
Table . S, 4 ). In all three patients, sampling was performed in the secondary surgery in case of oligometastatic 174 
recurrent disease. While two patients (#C865, #C218) had received chemotherapy and PARPi olaparib before 175 
the surgery , the third patient (#C 429) was treated wi th chemotherapy. Interestingly, p atient #C865 has 176

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
65, #C218) had received chemotherapy and PARPi olaparib before 175 
the surgery , the third patient (#C 429) was treated wi th chemotherapy. Interestingly, p atient #C865 has 176 
continued olaparib maintenance therapy after the surgery, and she is still showing a response with a stable 177 
disease (42 months) . Conversely, the other two patients, #C218 and #C 429, developed progressive disease 178 
after the surgery leading to death. 179 
The tumors collected from three patients at indicated time ( Fig. 3, A ) were processed for WES, scRNA -seq, 180 
and iPDC establishment for drug testing. The iPDCs from the patients exposed to chemotherapy and olaparib 181 
(#C865 , #C218_R (right ovary)) were treated with AZD6379 (ATRi), adavosertib (WEE1i), or olaparib (PARPi), 182

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
9 
 while the iPDCs from chemotherapy exposed patient (#C 429) were treated with AZD6379, adavosertib, or 183 
cisplatin for 3 -4 days. At the end of the drug t reatment, iPDCs were fixed and stained with anti -CK7 antibody 184 
to measure tumor cell abundance as a response to the drug treatment. Our results showed that olaparib, 185 
AZD6379, and adavosertib treatment resulted in a significant reduction in CK7+ tumor cells in the iPDCs from 186 
olaparib -responder patient (#C 865) compared to the control ( Fig. 3, B). Consistent with clinical responses, no 187 
significant reduction in CK7+ cells were detected following treatment with 10 uM olaparib or cisplatin in iPDCs 188 
from olaparib (#C218_R), or chemotherapy (#C429) resistant patients, respectively. Importantly, the iPDCs 189 
from the two resistant patients showed significant responses to ATRi, but not to WEE1i. Together, these results 190 
show that iPDCs recapitulate patient -specific clini cal responses to olaparib or chemotherapy and revealed 191 
ATR inhibition as a potential new treatment option for two of the therapy -resistant patients. 192 
To gain deeper insights into the molecular features driving drug sensitivity and resistance we performed 193 
scRNA -seq and WES on tumors from resistant patients. Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection 194

lecular features driving drug sensitivity and resistance we performed 193 
scRNA -seq and WES on tumors from resistant patients. Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection 194 
(UMAP) plots of all the cells passing the quality control of scRNA -seq data showed patient -specific as well as 195 
cell type -specific clusters ( Fig. 3, C and Fig. S3, A). In addition, epithelial cel sl and macrophages were the 196 
predominant cell types accounting for >70% of all cells identified with scRNA -seq ( Fig. 3, D). Epithelial cells 197 
progeny pathway scores per sample revealed that olaparib resistant tumor (#C218_ R) showed enrichment of 198 
estrogen , WNT, and TGFb pathways implicated in resistance to PARPi, in ovarian cancer (30-32), while 199 
chemotherapy resistant tumor (#C 429) wa s enriche d only in WNT signaling . On the contrary, TRAIL (tumor - 200 
necrosis factor related apoptosis -inducing ligand) pathway was enriched in the tumor from the olaparib - 201 
sensitive patient ( Fig. 3, E). Furthermore, cytotoxic T cells and macrophages from resistant tumors showed 202 
distinct activities related to WNT, and JAK -STAT signaling, respectively (Fig. S3, B and C). Next, we 203 
investigated if the sample -specific pathway enrichment s were genomically driven using the WES data from 204

JAK -STAT signaling, respectively (Fig. S3, B and C). Next, we 203 
investigated if the sample -specific pathway enrichment s were genomically driven using the WES data from 204 
two patients from which a diagnostic sample (pretreatment / before chemo theapy) was available ( #C865, and 205 
#C429). Exonic mutati ons from matched primary and resistant samples were analyzed specifically for TNF, 206 
EGFR, and PI3K -related pathways for patient #C865, and WNT -related pathways for patient #C429. However, 207 
we did not observe enrichment s of genomic mutations in these pathways , suggesting that the resistance 208 
associated pathway enrichments were driven mostly by cancer cell state dynamics (Table. S, 5). Previous 209 
reports show that a subset of PARPi resistant clones exhibiting enrichment of G2M Checkpoint, E2F targets 210 
are highly resistant to WEE1i (33). In line with these results, we found that olaparib and adavosertib resistant 211 
sample, #C218_R show enrichment of hallmark s of cell pr oliferation -related pathways such as, G2M 212

10 
 Checkpoint, E2F Targets, MYC Targets V1 compared to olaparib -sensitive tumor. Multiple mechanisms 213 
contributing to PARPi resistance have been identified , including restoration of the HR status, or BRCA1/2 214 
protein ex pression (33). The olaparib resistant patient (#C218) was diagnosed with a germline 215 
BRCA1:c.737delT mutation. However, somatic alterations analysis of the #C218 resistant tumor through WES 216 
revealed a deletion spanning from intron 6 to 7 to the middle of exon 10 on chromosome 17 (chr17:g.43092980 - 217 
43102933del) both in tumor and iPDCs , causing a potential in frame transcript and restoration of BRCA1 218 
activity. In add ition, functional HRD test (34) based on RAD51 foci on the formalin fixed tissue samples of 219 
these three tumors showed that the olaparib resistant #C218 sample was HRP while other two samples were 220 
found to be HRD, supporting the notion of HR restoration in #C218 . Together, the results from scRNA -seq 221 
and WES showing enrichment of pathways related to cell cycle, proliferation, and drug resistance, combined 222 
with restoration of HR proficiency suggest possible mechanisms of therapy resistance leading to progressive 223 
disease in olaparib resistant patient #C218. Collectively, our data suggests that integration of tumor genomic s, 224

mechanisms of therapy resistance leading to progressive 223 
disease in olaparib resistant patient #C218. Collectively, our data suggests that integration of tumor genomic s, 224 
transcriptomic s and drug response analyses from iPDCs can aid in identifying new therapeutic options and 225 
investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resi stant HGSC patients . 226 
iPDC platform identifies sensitivities to ATRi and immunotherapy combinations. 227 
We established a pipeline for testing the feasibility of using iPDCs for high -throughput combinatorial drug 228 
treatment as well as for comparative visualization of tumor or immune cell -specific drug response from multiple 229 
patients ( Fig. 4, A). We tested DNA damaging agents such as PARPi, ATRi, WEE1i, along with established 230 
immunotherapy agent, anti -PD1 antibody, as well as novel immunotherapies targeting the LPA, or PERK 231 
pathway , on 8 individual HGSC -iPDCs cultured in 384 well forma t (Table. S, ). Each 384 well plate consisted 232 
of 54 different conditions comprising 4 concentrations of single agents and 2 concentrations of combination 233 
treatments in a replicate of 4 wells. Drugs were dispensed in a randomized manner using a Tecan D300e drug 234 
dispenser. As a read out of the treatment resp onse, we employed four channel s IF staining followed by high - 235

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
dispensed in a randomized manner using a Tecan D300e drug 234 
dispenser. As a read out of the treatment resp onse, we employed four channel s IF staining followed by high - 235 
content imaging to measure single -cell cytotoxic response s from tumor (live / dead CK7+) and immune cells 236 
(live / dead CD45+). 237 
Unsupervised hierarchical clustering of live CK7+ tumor cells showed that samples clustered based on the 238 
drug treatment responses (Fig. S4 , A). Particularly, in 5/8 samples single agent ATRi or its combination with 239 
other agents was effective in reducing t he proportion of live CK7+ cells compared to control. Deeper 240 
investigation of the significance of this effect revealed that indeed ATRi single and combinatorial treatment 241

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
11 
 with other DNA damaging or immunotherapy agents cause significant tumor cell death in a sample -specific 242 
manner (4/8 iPDCs) ( Fig. 4, B). Notably , this effect was independent of tumor HR status. The analysis on 243 
CD45+ immune cell viability revealed that some of the samples showing tumor cell sensitivity to ATRi single 244 
and combination treatments also showed decrease in live CD45+ immune cells indicating that the treatment 245 
induces toxicity to normal immune cells . However, in two of the responding iPDCs (C053, C634), the anti - 246 
tumor effect s of ATRi combinations were higher than the cytotoxic effect on immune cells as the fold change 247 
in the reduction of live C K7+ cells was higher compared to live CD45+ cells . Furthermore, some of the samples 248 
showing sensitivity to ATRi combinations (C494, and C726) showed no significant change in the viability of 249 
immune cells, suggesting that the anti-tumor effect is superior to anti-immune cell effect in these samples. 250 
Conversely, in two samples (C564, C238) ATRi combinations increased the proportions of live CK7+ cells 251 
while decreasing the abundance of live CD45+ immune cells. In addition to ATRi single agent, WEE1i alone 252 
showed a decrease in tumor cell viability in 3 / 8 samples without significant effect s on the immune cell viability. 253

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ells. In addition to ATRi single agent, WEE1i alone 252 
showed a decrease in tumor cell viability in 3 / 8 samples without significant effect s on the immune cell viability. 253 
Again, this effect was independent of the tumor HR status (Fig. 4, B) . These results suggests that ATRi in 254 
combination with other DNA damaging agents: PARPi, or WEE1i, as well as immunotherapies: anti -PD1, ATXi, 255 
or PERKi shows effi cacy in HGSCs . Importantly, we show that assessment of immune cell -specific death 256 
response in addition to tumor cell -specific response, is necessary to evaluate the benefit of the immuno - 257 
oncology combinations. 258 
The results from our high -throughput combination treatments provide insights into the tumor -and immune cell - 259 
specific effect s of single agent ATRi and in combinations with other agents. Since our results suggested 260 
cytotoxic effect s of ATRi combinations on immune cells in a sample -specific mann er, we tested if lowering the 261 
concentrations of ATRi ha d beneficial effect s on immune cells yet cytotoxic for the cancer cells on iPDCs 262 
established from 6 new samples . We selected ATXi for further validation and functional analysis in combination 263 
with ATRi as this agent is known to induce CD8+ T cell activation via inducing interferon signaling in dendritic 264 
cells (9) .

vector_chunks.text:
dation and functional analysis in combination 263 
with ATRi as this agent is known to induce CD8+ T cell activation via inducing interferon signaling in dendritic 264 
cells (9) . Notably, 4/6 iPDCs showed sensitivity also to the combination of lower dose of ATRi with ATXi, 265 
without causing a significant reduction in live immune cells ( Fig. 4, C ). Importantly, this sensitivity associated 266 
with an increased CD8+T -cell activation indicated by higher GrzB , and interferon gamma (IFNγ ), along with 267 
dendritic cell activation with increased IFNγ in 3/4 of the sensitive iPDCs ( Fig. 4, D ). Together, these results 268 
confirm the efficacy an immune activation after ATRi plus ATXi combination treatment in HGSC and emph asize 269 
the added value of our iPDC platform to investigate both tumor cell -specific cytotoxic response and immune 270 
cell-specific functional states during immunotherapy combinations. 271

 Tumor -cell pRPA presents as a potential biomarker for ATRi plus ATXi combi nation treatment. 272 
Increase in replication stress markers such as phosphorylated replication stress protein A (RPA) has been 273 
suggested to be a predictive biomarker for sensitivity to ATRi (35). To evaluate if this is also reflected in the 274 
patient’s tumors, we performed tissue cyclic multiplex immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) (36) using antibodies 275 
against PAX8 (tumor cells), vimentin and replication stress marker pRPA32 -RPA2(Ser8) on the corresponding 276 
formalin -fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) tumor tissues from the same patients ( Fig. 4, B and C) . Single cell 277 
analysis of the tumor sa mples revealed that the majority of iPDCs responding the ATRi combinations were 278 
characterized by higher levels of pRPA32 in the tumor cells, as compared to the non -responding tumors ( Fig. 279 
4, E-F). Together, these results confirm the patient responses to co mbination of ATRi and immunotherapies 280 
and highlight the potential of our iPDC platform in screening of novel combinatorial treatments combined with 281 
investigation of tumor tissue -based predictive biomarkers. 282

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
13 
 DISCUSSION 283 
Here we established a novel patient -derived immunocompetent culture platform of HGSC tumors, and validate 284 
it using histologigy, genomic and single -cell analysis. Importantly, our results highlight that iPDCs mimic the 285 
clinical responses to PARPi , and chemotherapy treatments thus validating the utility of iPDCs for testing 286 
chemotherapy and targeted therapies. In line with the clinical responses to PARPi, we found increased tumor 287 
cell sensitivity of HRD -iPDCs to PARPi olaparib compared to HRP -iPDCs . Importantly, the in vitro iPDC 288 
response to PARPi significantly correlated with matched patient’s clinical responses particularly, PFS and the 289 
duration of PARPi treatment in the clinics. These results validate the utility of iPDCs for testing therapeutic 290 
agents from primary and therapy -resistant tumors. 291 
Tissue -specific matrices have been shown to improve the prediction of treatment responses by preserving the 292 
native ECM cues in pancreatic cancer and lymphoid metastases (21, 22 ). Previous studies on HGSC organoid 293 
cultures have mostly utilized mouse sarcoma derived BME matrix (37). Our result demonstrate that the unique 294 
OmGel provides an optimal growth environment recapitulating th e HGSC metastatic tumors. The proteomic 295

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ma derived BME matrix (37). Our result demonstrate that the unique 294 
OmGel provides an optimal growth environment recapitulating th e HGSC metastatic tumors. The proteomic 295 
analysis of OmGel by Neilson et al., report that there are no detectable levels of cytokines and growth factors 296 
in OmGel, suggesting that OmGel on its own does not alter the immune compartment of the iPDCs (23). In 297 
line with this we show that neither dispase digestion nor OmGel matrix alter the immune cells indicating that 298 
all the immune cells from the source tumors are faithfully recapitulated in the iPDCs, thereby making them an 299 
ideal model for the investigation of immunotherapy agents. As omentum is the most frequent site of 300 
intrabdominal metastases (38) our model with OmGel will be highly relevant for a broad range of 301 
gastrointestinal and gynecological cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum. 302 
Thus far, high -throughput drug response profiling in HGSC has been performed predominantly on cancer cell 303 
cultures or organoi ds using compounds targeting oncogenic drivers. Herein we have established a patient - 304 
derived pre -clinical model and analysis pipelines that facilitate the investigation of immuno -oncology 305 
combinations in a high -throughput manner enabling the analysis of tr eatment responses to over 50 different 306

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
elines that facilitate the investigation of immuno -oncology 305 
combinations in a high -throughput manner enabling the analysis of tr eatment responses to over 50 different 306 
conditions on tumors from multiple patients. Moreover, while previous studies have mainly involved 307 
measurement of metabolic markers independent of the cell types, our single -cell image -based readout 308 
uniquely captures tumor and immune cell specific functional states. Although the evidence from clinical trials 309 
indicates unfavorable responses to ICIs in HGSC, these studies were conducted on unstratified patient 310 
populations (39). Here we provide novel insights that iPDCs established from treatment -naive tumors show 311

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
14 
 tumor cell responses to pembrolizumab predominantly in HRD -iPDCs. However, none of our patients received 312 
ICIs and thus the identification of HGSC patients potentially responding to ICIs still needs further investigation. 313 
Although combination of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents such as PARPi, or WEE1i have been clinically 314 
tested in ovarian cancer (40, 41 ), the combination of ATRi with immunotherapy has not been investigated 315 
before. For the first time, we report that combination of ATRi with both investigational (ATXi, and PERKi), and 316 
established (anti -PD1 antibody) immunotherapies show patient -specific effects on tumor and immune cell 317 
viability. Furthermore, we found that higher concentrations (1µM or above) of ATRi are likely toxic as they 318 
caused both cancer and immune cell death in a subset of iPDCs. However, our results using lower 319 
concentrations of ATRi (0.1µM) in combination w ith ATXi showed cancer cell -specific death without affecting 320 
the immune cell viability in a tumor -dependent manner. Importantly, tumor cell -death responses to this 321 
combination aligned with CD8+ T cell and dendritic cell activation. Together our results hig hlight ATRi plus 322 
immunotherapies as a promising new combination treatment and suggest that iPDCs are suitable for screening 323

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
tic cell activation. Together our results hig hlight ATRi plus 322 
immunotherapies as a promising new combination treatment and suggest that iPDCs are suitable for screening 323 
immuno -oncology compounds as well as for deeper analysis of functional immune -cell state dynamics. 324 
Our iPDC platform also enables identification of potential biomarkers for response and resistance to 325 
treatments. Combining the drug response from treatment resistant iPDCs with pathway analysis from scRNA - 326 
seq and mutation analysis from WES suggested possible mechanism of resistance to P ARPi. These include 327 
enrichment of cell cycle, G2M checkpoint, WNT, and TGFb pathways in PARPi resistant tumor, an acquired 328 
BRCA1 mutation likely leading to restoration of HR function. These findings support the concept of integrating 329 
drug sensitivity and r esistance profiling of iPDCs with molecular features from the corresponding tumors to 330 
reveal mechanism behind treatment responses to enable implementation of more effective treatment 331 
strategies. Furthermore, the results from t -CycIF corroborate previous st udies showing replication stress as a 332 
biomarker of sensitivity to ATRi and suggest that this approach aid in the investigation tissue -based biomarkers 333 
for potential combination therapies identified on iPDCs.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
332 
biomarker of sensitivity to ATRi and suggest that this approach aid in the investigation tissue -based biomarkers 333 
for potential combination therapies identified on iPDCs. Our data from iPDCs highlight that integration o f high - 334 
content single cell image analysis with immune cell functional states from flow cytometry enable assessment 335 
of tumor cell -specific cytotoxic and immune cell -specific functional responses for stratification into responders 336 
and non -responders. Togethe r, our novel HGSC patient -derived immunocompetent cultures in physiologically 337 
relevant matrix combined with patient -specific tumor infiltrating immune cells facilitates high -throughput testing 338 
of novel immuno -oncology agents and personalized combination th erapies in HGSC. 339

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
15 
 The limitations of iPDCs include that they are short -term cultures lasting up to one week without the ability to 340 
passage over time. This is inherent to any relevant immuno -oncology ex vivo models that can sustain the 341 
viability and function al states of the diverse immune cell repertoire. Secondly, this platform only allows 342 
investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra -tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of 343 
the peripheral blood immune cells. Thirdly, the number of PARPi and resistant patients in our study is limited, 344 
due to the fact that these samples are difficult to get, and thus further validations using a larger number of 345 
recurrent samples are warranted. Despite these limitations, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient -matched 346 
intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno -oncology 347 
agents in activating intra -tumoral immune cells. Furthermore, our work highlight ATRi plus immunotherapy 348 
combinations as pote ntially effective agents in HGSC and demonstrate that coupling of iPDC drug responses 349 
with molecular and tissue -based features enable the discovery of predictive biomarkers. 350

 MATERIALS AND METHODS 351 
Patient material and clinical data 352 
All experiments involvi ng patient materials were performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 353 
1975 Declaration of Helsinki. Tissue samples were collected as a part of ONCOSYS -OVA study (clinical trial 354 
number : NCT061173 84) from HGSC patients undergoing primary debu lking surgery (PDS) (n=41) or 355 
secondary surgery (n=3) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). Each patient gave written informed consent 356 
and the use of fresh tissue, blood, and formalin -fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) HGSC clinical material was 357 
approved by the Ethics Committee of Women’s Clinic, HUH and Helsin ki Biobank. 358 
Tumor tissue processing 359 
In this study, w e processed HGSC tumors from 39 patients, depending on the availability tumor tissues were 360 
subjected to different experimental procedures listed in Table. S , 1. Freshly resected tumor tissue was cut into 361 
multiple pieces (approximately 100 mm3 per piece) . Three to six pieces were snap frozen in liquid nitrogen for 362 
DNA, and RNA, isolation, one piece was fixed in 10% formalin for histopathology, and t -CycIF analysis. 363 
Depending on the availability 100 mm3-2000 mm3 cm tumor tissue piece was minced into smaller pieces and 364

GlutaMAX™ -I (100X) (#35050 -061, GibcoLifeTechnologies), Penicillin -Streptomycin (10,000 U/ml), 367 
(#15140122 GibcoLifeTechnologies), and HEPES, (# H0887, Sigma)), and 50 U/ mL DNase (# M6101, 368 
Promega) at 37°C for 1 hr rotating incubator. Digested tiss ue was filtered through 70µM strainer and 369 
centrifuged at 1000 RPM for 10 min at +4°C . The pellet was subjected to RBC lysis for 5 min at room 370 
temperature (RT) followed by dilution in AdF+++ medium and centrifuged at 1000 RPM for 10 min at +4°C . 371 
Cells in th e resulting final pellet was counted with trypan blue using LUNA -FL™ Dual Fluorescence Cell 372 
Counter. 373

Scientific) at a concentration of 30000 cells / 3µL and allowed to air dry at RT. Cells were stained with 376

Patient -derived OmGel was prepared as previously described (23). HGSC tumor -dissociated cells were mixed 390 
with Cultrex RGF Basement Membrane Extract (BME), Type 2 (# 3533 -010-02 R&D Systems), or 2.5 mg/mL 391

vector_chunks.text:
SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS 1 
WES data processing and analysis 2 
Bioinformatic processing of DNA samples involved several steps, including quality control, alignment to 3 
the reference human genome, deduplication, cross -sample contamination estimation, and variant 4 
discovery. Sequenced DNA reads underwent quality control a nd trimming steps using FastQC (47) and 5 
Trimmomatic tools (48). Subsequently, the high -quality reads were aligned to the reference human 6 
genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA -MEM (49) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with 7 
Picard tool and base quality recalibration using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) version 4.1.9.0 8 
(50). Cross -sample contamination estimation was conducted using GATK 4.1.9.0, with a contamination 9 
estimation threshold established at 15%. 10 
 11 
Mutation calling 12 
Somatic short variants were called through the collective analysis of multiple tumor samples against a 13 
singular matched normal for each patient, using GATK 4.1.4.1 Mutect2 according to established best 14 
practices. We used a Panel of Normal (PoN) generated with 181 normal samples from the prospective, 15 
longitudinal, multiregion observational study DECIDER (Multi -Layer Data to Improve Diagnosis, Predict 16 
Therapy Resistance and Suggest Targeted Therapies in HGSOC; ClinicalTrials.gov identifier 17

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
gion observational study DECIDER (Multi -Layer Data to Improve Diagnosis, Predict 16 
Therapy Resistance and Suggest Targeted Therapies in HGSOC; ClinicalTrials.gov identifier 17 
NCT04846933) and 99 TCGA normals. A detailed description of PoN creation can be found in Lahtinen 18 
et al (51). Subsequently, GATK FilterMutectCalls was used for variant filtration, keeping only those 19 
variants that successfully passing all filters. Variant allele frequencies (VAF) were computed based on 20 
the read depths for reference and alternat e alleles (AD field). We annotated the using ANNOVAR 21 
20191024 (52). 22 
 23 
iPDC -tissue concordance evaluation 24 
To assess the alignment between iPDC and tissue samples, we calculated tissue -based concordance 25 
(Ct) by determining the ratio of shared mutations in both iPDC (i) and tissue samples (t) to mutations 26 
found in the tissue (t) Ct = (Mi ∩ Mt) / Mt. Concordance analysis was conducted for iPDC -tissue pairs, 27 
with each pair consisting of iPDC and tissue samples derived from the same patient and organ. In total, 28 
10 iPDC -tissue pairs were analyzed. 29 
 30

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
iPDC -tissue VAF correlation 31 
Like iPDC -tissue concordance, we computed organoid -tissue Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) 32 
correlation for 10 pairs composed of iPDC and tissue samples from the same patient. 33 
 34 
Mutation and pathway analysis of diagnostic and resistant samples 35 
Exonic mutations were co llected from both primary and relapse samples for patients C865 and C429 . 36 
The mutated genes identified from these samples were then subjected to over -representation analysis 37 
using the ConsensusPathDB algorithm (5 3). 38 
 39

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/Drug Treatment Plotting Notes/Drug Plotting Notes_.docx
vector_chunks.text: To distinguish between the intensity of these two cell populations the integrated intensity, which accounts for the size of the cell, is recorded for the tumor cells. Since tumor cells are larger the intensity values for the tumor cells are also greater allowing for gating by intensity

vector_chunks.text: Most of the double positives in S141 were dead and the double positives cells (CK7+ and CD45+) were not visible in the images. How do we approach categorizing these cells?

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/Drug Treatment Plotting Notes/Drug Plotting Notes_.docx
vector_chunks.text: I’m Aditi Sirsikar, a master’s student in Anniina Farkkila’s lab. We have HGSC patient-derived immunocompetent cultures in a humanized matrix. We would like to run scRNA sequencing on these samples.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/Drug Treatment Plotting Notes/Drug Plotting Notes_.docx
vector_chunks.text: Describe how the samples have been stored. These are the control samples, they are not treated with any drugs only DMSO 2 and the purpose of the experiments is to evaluate whether the cultured sample recapitulates main gene expression signatures as the original (ex. Vivo = out of the living) parental tumor and to tease out pathways up and down regulated in the tumor facilitating growth of the tumor to provide an explanation for the drug responses plotted earlier.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/Drug Treatment Plotting Notes/Drug Plotting Notes_.docx
vector_chunks.text: We have a few questions about potential interference from foreign rna from the gel matrix derived from human tissue (pooled omentum tissue from multiple patient samples)

vector_chunks.text: Below please find the point-by-point rebuttal letter addressing all the Reviewer’s comments.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: Reviewer: 1

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: This manuscript describes a novel approach to high throughput in vitro drug screening in patient-derived organoids from women with ovarian cancer. The authors describe advantages to this approach including the ability to evaluate treatment effects on both tumor and immune cell populations, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response. Strengths of the manuscript include the attention to potential confounding effects of culture conditions with the observation that tissue dissociation methods could induce a stress response associated with chemoresistance, the analysis of tumor samples collected at secondary debulking to evaluate treatment resistant samples, and the quantification of treatment effects on immune cells as a potential toxicity to consider in establishing dosing. Weaknesses of this report are primarily related to the small sample sizes and the lack of clinical correlation for validation in most cases. Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment. This approach requires substantial clinical validation prior to advancing the model as a strategy to assign patients to treatment regimens.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: We thank the reviewer for constructive feedback on our manuscript and highlighting the strengths of this study.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: Specific issues that should be addressed by the authors prior to publication include:

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: There is clinical correlation for PARPi treatment efficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. The association between HRD and PARPi sensitivity in vitro is well established, but results in patients are more complex, particularly with evidence that PARPi have immunomodulatory effects. It is unclear whether the inclusion of an immune component in the iPDC can improve prediction beyond tumor specific in vitro cultures without a comparison with patient data for validation.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: We acknowledge that the numbers were small in the original manuscript. We have now collected clinical data for PARPi and or platinum sensitivity from 20 patients which will be correlated with responses from corresponding iPDCs. Given 3 months time for the revisions, we can provide analyses of up to XX samples significantly strengthening the conclusions of the manuscript.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: While it is true that the pro and anti-tumor immunomodulatory effects of PARPi have been established (REF) through migration and activation of immune cells and by impairing DNA damage in immune cells and upregulation of PD-L1 on tumor cells. We focused on validating the iPDC model by evaluating the effect of PARPi on cancer cells to clinically validated / predicted HRD-specific anti-tumor response. However, we have now also performed flow cytometry analysis from PARPi treated iPDCs. From this data we can analyze activation states of T cells, Dendritic cells, and NK cells to evaluate immune cell-specific responses to PARPi. We can provide this result during the revision stage along with the clinical correlation to PARPi sensitivity.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories. While two are described as having progressed on PARPi and chemo, it’s not clear if they would still be considered platinum sensitive based on the diagram in Fig 3A or platinum resistant. Response to PARPi correlates with platinum sensitivity so this would be helpful information to have. Additionally, there is no description of what chemo regimens they received or why they underwent secondary debulking-which is relatively uncommon after 2-3 recurrences, particularly in the setting of disease resistant to multiple chemo regimens. Furthermore, it appears that the investigators do not have data from pre-treatment samples from these same patients to enable them to identify changes associated with chemoresistance – they cite changes in estrogen, TGFb, WNT as being implicated in resistance but don’t have information about baseline expression. Finally, it does not appear that these patients received any of the tested agents with the exception of the patient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: atient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses. Ultimately, the data in Fig 3 and the analysis of drug sensitivities in resistant tumors present detailed descriptions of three cases but are not sufficient to support the conclusion that “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: The three recurrent samples analyzed in this manuscript are representative cases to evaluate if iPDCs can mimic clinically known drug resistance, as well as guide in the identification of effective drugs or combinations in the relapse settings. These three patients underwent secondary debulking surgeries due to oligometastatic disease, and due to no prior debulking surgery. The two patients #C218 and #C429 progressed on PARPi and chemotherapy, respectively they were considered as platinum sensitive base on the primary response to platinum-free interval (PFI) which was 24 months (#C218) and 20 months (#C429). Although they showed initial sensitivity to platinum and PARPi, later developed progressive disease. We will provide a detailed supplementary table for chemotherapy regimens and response table for these three patients.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: The pre-treatment samples are available for 2 of the patients - and we have performed WES on the pretreatment samples. During the revisions we will perform tCycIF single-cell spatial analysis of the pretreatment samples - to investigate the tumor-immune landscapes of the pre- and post treatment samples including ER, TGFb, and WNT signaling.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: We acknowledge that the conclusion of “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients” is not sufficient based on three samples. Hence, we will tone down our statement to “iPDCs may aid in the investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text: Although the emphasis here is on tumor-intrinsic factors like cytotoxicity, the innovation of the iPDC model is the ability to assess immunologic effects of therapy and the efficacy of immune therapy – tumor extrinsic effects. Because the iPDC can only be maintained for a short time, the analyses of effects on immune cells are restricted to immediate effects on pre-existing tumor infiltrating or tumor-associated lymphocytes. Other mechanisms contributing to the efficacy of immune therapy, such as activation of new T cell populations recognizing neoantigens or changes in T cell recruitment to tumor sites, would be missed in the iPDC system. Induction of immune memory also cannot be assessed.

vector_chunks.text: As the reviewer pointed out, the limitation of iPDCs is that it only allows investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra-tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of the peripheral blood immune cells. Despite this limitation, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient-matched intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno-oncology agents in activating intra-tumoral immune cells. We will include this point in the Discussion.

vector_chunks.text: The TOPACIO trial is cited as a justification for investigations of HRD tumors. It is important to note that the trial tested combined PARPi and PD1 ICI, not PD1 ICI monotherapy, and no correlation between tumor HRD status and response was observed in patients. These data and other clinical trial results showing limited efficacy of PD1 ICI are difficult to reconcile with the comparatively high response to PD1 ICI in iPDC. The authors suggest that this may be because patients receive PD1 after prior chemo, but frontline ICI combination trials in OC have also shown limited benefit. This discrepancy highlights the need for additional translational validation of this model system prior to implementation.

vector_chunks.text: Note: Everyone agreed that this paper was well written and easy to follow. We discussed designing models specific to the questions we aim to investigate. The functional precision medicine platform described in this paper is a short-term patient derived culture very similar to the iPDCs established in our lab. However, the fPM model focused on developing organoids composed of tumor and stromal cells while disregarding immune cells. We discussed that this a significant shortcoming given the potential synergistic effect of DNA damaging drugs (DDD) and immunotherapies. In addition, the exclusion immune cells may underestimate the cytotoxic off-target effect of DDD combinations. Moving forward in the iPDC project, we aim to add fibroblasts to our cultures to study the interplay of tumor, immune, and stromal cells in an ex vivo model. In addition, we strive to test more combination therapies at different concentrations to identify effective tumor-specific novel therapies and combination therapies.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx
vector_chunks.text: This manuscript describes a novel approach to high throughput in vitro drug screening in patient-derived organoids from women with ovarian cancer. The authors describe advantages to this approach including the ability to evaluate treatment effects on both tumor and immune cell populations, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response. Strengths of the manuscript include the attention to potential confounding effects of culture conditions with the observation that tissue dissociation methods could induce a stress response associated with chemoresistance, the analysis of tumor samples collected at secondary debulking to evaluate treatment resistant samples, and the quantification of treatment effects on immune cells as a potential toxicity to consider in establishing dosing. Weaknesses of this report are primarily related to the small sample sizes and the lack of clinical correlation for validation in most cases. Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx
vector_chunks.text: Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment. vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx
vector_chunks.text: While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories. While two are described as having progressed on PARPi and chemo, it’s not clear if they would still be considered platinum sensitive based on the diagram in Fig 3A or platinum resistant. Response to PARPi correlates with platinum sensitivity so this would be helpful information to have. Additionally, there is no description of what chemo regimens they received or why they underwent secondary debulking-which is relatively uncommon after 2-3 recurrences, particularly in the setting of disease resistant to multiple chemo regimens. Furthermore, it appears that the investigators do not have data from pre-treatment samples from these same patients to enable them to identify changes associated with chemoresistance – they cite changes in estrogen, TGFb, WNT as being implicated in resistance but don’t have information about baseline expression. Finally, it does not appear that these patients received any of the tested agents with the exception of the patient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses.

vector_chunks.text: Although the emphasis here is on tumor-intrinsic factors like cytotoxicity, the innovation of the iPDC model is the ability to assess immunologic effects of therapy and the efficacy of immune therapy – tumor extrinsic effects. vector_chunks.text: The experiments are performed in a short time frame - whereby the effects of only direct toxicities of the drugs to the different cell populations (tumor-immune cells) in the iPDCs. Therefore - our approach is focused on the tumor-specific effects of the treatments. This limitation will be further clarified in the Manuscript and Discussion.

vector_chunks.text: The TOPACIO trial is cited as a justification for investigations of HRD tumors. It is important to note that the trial tested combined PARPi and PD1 ICI, not PD1 ICI monotherapy, and no correlation between tumor HRD status and response was observed in patients. These data and other clinical trial results showing limited efficacy of PD1 ICI are difficult to reconcile with the comparatively high response to PD1 ICI in iPDC. The authors suggest that this may be because patients receive PD1 after prior chemo, but frontline ICI combination trials in OC have also shown limited benefit. This discrepancy highlights the need for additional translational validation of this model system prior to implementation.

vector_chunks.source_file: 8_iPDC_1.0/ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx
vector_chunks.text: In our work by Färkkilä et al., describing the translational work on the TOPACIO clincial trial, we demonstrated a significant correlation of HRD and PFS to the combination of PARPi + PD-1 ICI (2).

vector_chunks.text: Frontline ICI combination trials in ovarian cancer are still waiting for results: currently, four large Phase III clinical trials are ongoing (ENGOT-OV43/KEYLYNK-001, DUO-O, ATHENA-COMBO, ENGOT-OV44/ FIRST) investigating the efficacy of 1.st line IO + chemo combinations, with the first results expected to be released in Q2/2025. Therefore, it is currently unclear to which patients ICI should be administered in ovarian cancer, highlighting the timeliness of our efforts in identification of patients responding to ICI.
